planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, sandy or rocky, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade. Silver Thatch Palm is highly drought- and salt-tolerant and is excellent for the thin, rocky, alkaline soils of the Florida Keys where other palms struggle.

directions: Silver Thatch Palm is a graceful, slender native palm, native to the rocky, alkaline limestone soils of the Florida Keys, extreme southern Miami-Dade County, and the Caribbean, where it grows in coastal hammocks and pine rocklands as one of the most salt- and drought-tolerant palms in the Florida native flora. Its most distinctive feature is the brilliant silver-white coloration of the undersides of the fan-shaped fronds, which flash dramatically in the breeze and give the tree a shimmering, two-toned effect unlike any other native Florida palm. Slow-growing but long-lived, Silver Thatch Palm produces small white flowers in summer followed by round, dark purple berries consumed by birds; historically the leaves were used for thatching (hence the common name). An outstanding specimen palm for the thin, rocky, alkaline marl and limestone soils of South Florida gardens where faster-growing palms would quickly exhaust the substrate — plant in full sun to partial shade, provide excellent drainage, and supply minimal supplemental water once established.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun. California Fan Palm is more cold-tolerant than Mexican Fan Palm (Washingtonia robusta) and can survive brief dips to 15–18°F when established; avoid wet, poorly drained soils.

directions: California Fan Palm is the only palm native to the contiguous western United States, native to natural oases in the desert canyons and spring-fed washes of the Sonoran and Mojave Deserts in southern California and far western Arizona, where it forms dramatic palm oases — some of the most distinctive ecosystems in North America — around underground water sources. Stouter and shorter than its close relative Mexican Fan Palm (Washingtonia robusta) and more cold-hardy (to about 15°F), California Fan Palm features large, bold, gray-green fan-shaped fronds with characteristic hair-like threads (fila) hanging from the leaf segments and retains a thick skirt of hanging dead fronds that provides nesting habitat for orioles, house sparrows, and exotic monk parakeets. The small, dark fruits are an important food source for desert wildlife and were historically an important food for indigenous peoples of the Southwest. Grow in full sun in any well-drained soil — it is extremely drought-tolerant once established and requires minimal care beyond occasional irrigation to maintain appearance.

planting.note: Tuck nursery transplants into crevices of acidic rock (sandstone or granite preferred, not limestone) in a shaded to partially shaded rock garden, with excellent drainage and consistent humidity. Mountain Spleenwort is challenging to grow outside of natural rock environments and performs best when the root zone is sheltered within a tight rock crevice.

directions: Mountain Spleenwort is a small, neat, evergreen native fern, native to the shaded, acidic rock outcrops, cliff faces, and boulder crevices of the Appalachian Mountains from New England south to Georgia and Alabama, where it colonizes the smallest cracks in sandstone and granite in the deep shade of north-facing cliffs. Its triangular, twice-pinnate fronds — reaching just 4–10 inches — are dark green, leathery, and persistent through winter, and the plant forms compact tufted rosettes anchored tightly in rock crevices with minimal root access to soil. Unlike the Maidenhair Spleenwort (Asplenium trichomanes), which prefers limestone or neutral-alkaline rock, Mountain Spleenwort is strictly an acidic-rock specialist, growing naturally on sandstone, granite, and quartzite but not on calcium-rich rocks. Most challenging to establish in cultivation outside of natural rock garden conditions — it is best planted directly into a tight crevice in an acidic rock garden or dry stone wall built with sandstone, where the roots can remain sheltered and consistently humid even as the fronds dry rapidly in the open air.
sources: Native Ferns, Mosses and Grasses (William Cullina)

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in wet to moist, slightly acidic, sandy or loamy soil in partial shade to full sun. Corkwood is a rare species that should only be sourced from specialty native plant nurseries; it is listed as threatened or of conservation concern in most states where it occurs.

directions: Corkwood is one of the rarest and most botanically unusual native shrubs in North America — a monotypic genus (Leitneriaceae family) with no close relatives among living plants, native to the tidal swamps, brackish marshes, and wet bottomlands of scattered, disjunct localities in Georgia, Florida, Texas, Arkansas, and Missouri, where it forms thickets in periodically flooded, organic soils. Its most remarkable characteristic is its wood, which is lighter than cork — making it the least dense wood of any known North American woody plant — and the plant occupies a phylogenetically isolated position in the evolutionary tree of flowering plants. Catkin-like male and female flowers appear on separate plants in spring before the leaves emerge. Despite its rarity, Corkwood is adaptable to a range of wet to moist soil conditions in partial shade to full sun and is occasionally available from specialty native plant nurseries; plant male and female specimens together for seed production. Listed as threatened in several states and on NatureServe's watch list.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in well-drained, acidic, sandy soil in full sun, pressing seeds into the soil surface; germination occurs in spring. Transplant container-grown nursery trees in fall or early spring for best establishment. Slash Pine grows extremely fast and should be given ample space.

directions: Slash Pine is the dominant pine of the Gulf Coast and Florida peninsula, native to the wet flatwoods, mesic pinelands, and pond edges of the southeastern coastal plain from South Carolina to Florida and west to Louisiana, where it forms the foundational canopy of the longleaf pine savanna ecosystem alongside longleaf and pond pines. One of the fastest-growing native pines — typically adding 3–5 feet per year in good conditions — Slash Pine is widely planted for timber, pulpwood, and naval stores (resin/turpentine), and in Florida it is a primary host tree for the red-cockaded woodpecker and the state champion in terms of longleaf pine savanna ecosystem support. Two varieties occur in Florida: var. elliottii (North Florida and coastal plain, tall and fast-growing) and var. densa (South Florida Slash Pine, shorter, denser, more fire-resistant). Plant in full sun in acidic, well-drained to moderately moist sandy soil; it is intolerant of alkaline conditions, compaction, or prolonged drought once young.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, loamy soil in partial shade to full sun. Spruce Pine is ecologically unique among eastern pines in its ability to establish and grow in the partial shade of mixed hardwood forests — the only pine native to the Southeast that tolerates significant shade.

directions: Spruce Pine is the most ecologically unusual of the southeastern native pines — the only pine in eastern North America that regularly grows as an understory component of bottomland hardwood forests and shaded mixed woods, native to the coastal plain and lower Piedmont from South Carolina to northern Florida and west to Louisiana, where it occurs in moist, fertile, loamy soils in the shade of sweetgum, water oak, and magnolia rather than in the open pinelands where other southeastern pines grow. Its smooth, gray bark (glabra means smooth or glabrous) is distinctive among pines and gives the tree an appearance reminiscent of a smooth-barked hardwood; the short, clustered needles and small cones distinguish it from other southeastern species. A valuable native pine for moist, shaded woodland gardens and naturalistic landscapes in zones 7–9 where its unusual shade tolerance sets it apart from all other native conifers. Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; it is not drought-tolerant and requires consistently moist conditions.
sources: Flora of the Southeastern United States (Weakley)

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in fall or early spring in any well-drained soil in full sun; Eastern Red Cedar is extremely adaptable and tolerates poor, thin, rocky, dry, alkaline, and compacted soils where few other trees will grow. Female plants produce berries only when male plants are nearby.

directions: Eastern Red Cedar is the most widely distributed conifer in eastern North America, native from Nova Scotia to Florida and west to the Great Plains, colonizing abandoned fields, roadsides, fence lines, and rocky glades with a tenacity matched by few other native trees — it is the quintessential first colonizer of open, disturbed ground in eastern North America and has expanded its range dramatically following suppression of natural fire and the abandonment of agricultural fields. The aromatic, reddish-brown wood was historically prized for cedar chests, pencils, and fence posts due to its rot-resistance and insect-repelling properties; the fleshy, glaucous-blue, seed-bearing 'cones' (botanically berries) are an essential winter food for cedar waxwings, bluebirds, mockingbirds, and dozens of other birds, and the dense, scale-like foliage provides critical winter nesting cover. Eastern Red Cedar is a host for the alternate life stage of cedar-apple rust (Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae), which can infect nearby apple trees — avoid planting within 1,000 feet of apple orchards. An extremely tough, drought-tolerant, adaptable native tree for landscapes from zone 2–9.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in fall or early spring in well-drained, sandy or loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun. Southern Red Cedar is highly salt-tolerant and is an excellent windbreak and wildlife tree for coastal landscapes in the Gulf states.

directions: Southern Red Cedar is the coastal plain and Gulf Coast counterpart to Eastern Red Cedar (Juniperus virginiana), native to the sandy soils, coastal hammocks, maritime forests, and barrier islands from North Carolina through Florida and around the Gulf Coast to Texas, where it is distinguished from J. virginiana by its more pendulous (weeping) branch tips and overall softer appearance. Salt spray-tolerant, drought-tolerant, and adapted to the sandy, low-nutrient soils of the coastal plain, Southern Red Cedar is an excellent native windbreak, privacy screen, and wildlife tree for Gulf Coast landscapes in zones 7–11. Like Eastern Red Cedar, it produces fleshy blue-green berries (technically seed cones) that are consumed by dozens of bird species including cedar waxwings and bluebirds, and provides dense, year-round nesting cover; plant male and female trees together for consistent berry production. Grow in full sun in any well-drained soil; it is notably more heat-tolerant than Eastern Red Cedar and performs better in the lower South and in coastal situations.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in wet to moist, acidic, slightly sandy or loamy soil in full sun to partial shade. Swamp Tupelo is adapted to seasonally or permanently flooded conditions and develops distinctive swollen (buttressed) trunk bases in very wet soils; it is most appropriate for rain gardens, wet bottomlands, and natural pond margins.

directions: Swamp Tupelo is a dominant native tree of the permanent swamps, bay forests, and river floodplains of the southeastern coastal plain from Delaware to Florida and west to Texas, often growing alongside bald cypress and water tupelo in pure stands in the deepest, most permanently flooded low-lying land in the South. It is distinguished from the more upland Black Gum (Nyssa sylvatica) by its narrower leaves, preference for wetter conditions, and more southern distribution; in very wet sites it develops the distinctive swollen, buttressed trunk base characteristic of swamp-adapted trees. In fall Swamp Tupelo produces one of the finest foliage displays of any native tree — brilliant crimson, scarlet, and orange — providing spectacular autumn color even in the deep South where fall foliage is often muted. Small blue-black drupes in fall are consumed by a wide variety of birds and mammals. Plant in consistently wet to seasonally flooded, acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; it tolerates standing water better than almost any other native upland tree.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty soil in full sun; the serotinous cones require heat (fire or prolonged heating) to release seeds naturally, so for cultivation purposes collect cones and gently heat them in an oven (250°F for 30 minutes) to open. Pond Pine establishes most reliably in its natural wet, acidic habitat.

directions: Pond Pine is an ecologically fascinating native pine, the dominant tree of the pocosins (shrubby Carolina bogs) and wet pine flatwoods of the southeastern coastal plain from New Jersey to northern Florida, where it grows in the most permanently saturated, acidic, peaty soils of the coastal plain in full sun — a habitat as harsh as any in the temperate world. Its cones are strongly serotinous — sealed shut with resin — and open most reliably after fire, giving Pond Pine a remarkable fire-dependent life history: the dense, wet habitat rarely burns, but when lightning does ignite a pocosin, the heat opens the cones and the charred, open landscape gives the fire-adapted seedlings their best chance for establishment. Pond Pine sprouts vigorously from the root crown after fire, regenerating rapidly even when top-killed. In natural landscape and restoration plantings, Pond Pine is appropriate for the wet, boggy, acidic conditions of pocosins and seepage bogs in zones 6–9, where its tolerance for waterlogged soils matches or exceeds that of any other eastern pine species.
